caveats: a: Labor shares cover all U.S. agriculture rather than sugar-beet farms.
a: Precision-adoption evidence is not crop-specific to sugar beets.
a: The frozen labor-share input is missing, so the primitive cannot be combined with a measured 111991 labor intensity.
rho: No five-year AI implementation study specific to sugar-beet farming was found.
rho: Adoption and implementation vary sharply by farm scale and processor technology requirements.
e: Acreage and farm counts are not equivalent to LMM operating firms.
e: Production contracts govern crop sale but do not establish service eligibility.
s5: Farm succession does not necessarily transfer a recurring customer-service business.
s5: Processor delivery rights and cooperative interests may not transfer with operating control.
q: Crop purchase contracts are not equivalent to outsourced-service billing.
q: Policy-supported sugar pricing and cooperative patronage can obscure sharing of operating efficiencies.
d5: Beet acreage and sugar output do not directly measure outsourced operating-service demand.
d5: Weather, planting delays, storage shrink, sucrose recovery, imports, and policy can dominate annual results.
d5: The five-year service base is unverified.
o: The estimate separates generative AI from mechanization and autonomous equipment.
o: The accountable outsourced-operator population itself remains unverified.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The qualifying model would use recurring farm-operation, crop-share, acreage, or management agreements with responsibility for a full beet-growing program. Sugar-beet production commonly uses processor-linked crop sales contracts, but those contracts coordinate commodity sale and are not evidence of outsourced operating-service revenue.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to repeat outsourced farm operation because 111991 is a crop-production industry, while the fixed lens requires recurring outsourced service. Processor production contracts are common but do not by themselves convert a grower into a service provider.
lens.excluded_activities: Owner-operated beet farms selling their own crop under purchase or marketing contracts, passive land and financing vehicles, manage-only agricultural services classified in NAICS 115, beet processing and sugar manufacturing, non-sugar beet farming, and one-off custom fieldwork.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms, if any, providing repeat outsourced operation and management of sugar-beet production for external landowners or program customers while materially operating the farm.

a.bridge: USDA all-farm occupation shares and field-crop precision-technology adoption are combined with ILO broad-occupation GenAI exposure to infer wage-weighted current opportunity for sugar-beet operations. This can overstate exposure because field and equipment work dominates and understate it because agronomy, processor coordination, logistics, and owner-management labor are incompletely captured.
caveats: Labor shares cover all U.S. agriculture rather than sugar-beet farms.
Precision-adoption evidence is not crop-specific to sugar beets.
The frozen labor-share input is missing, so the primitive cannot be combined with a measured 111991 labor intensity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study for sugar-beet farms, including owner labor, hired labor, current guidance and variable-rate use, storage and harvest coordination, and processor-facing administration, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can assist crop and harvest planning, weather and disease interpretation, input purchasing, processor scheduling, records, compliance, and equipment support. Tillage, planting, spraying, irrigation, lifting, hauling, maintenance, and storage management remain physical, with guidance and precision tools already automating part of field execution.

d5.bridge: USDA sugar-beet acreage, beet-sugar output, and total sugar-use forecasts are translated into year-five demand for the hypothetical outsourced farm-operation basket. This can overstate the lens if reduced acreage remains owner-operated and understate it if processor consolidation or land concentration increases third-party operating mandates.
caveats: Beet acreage and sugar output do not directly measure outsourced operating-service demand.
Weather, planting delays, storage shrink, sucrose recovery, imports, and policy can dominate annual results.
The five-year service base is unverified.
d5.change_evidence: USDA Baseline beet acreage and sugar-use projections, processor slicing capacity and closures, delivery-right changes, land leases, and verified outsourced operating acreage would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: USDA reported 2025 sugar-beet production of 35.14 million tons, while June 2026 intended planted acreage was 1.033 million acres versus 1.079 million in 2025. The May outlook projected 2026/27 beet-sugar output at its lowest since 2019/20, although total U.S. sugar use remains substantial and policy-managed supply supports continued domestic production.

caveats: Acreage and farm counts are not equivalent to LMM operating firms.
Production contracts govern crop sale but do not establish service eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A verified farm-to-firm roster with normalized EBITDA, land ownership, processor and operating contracts, revenue source, customer recurrence, control eligibility, and NAICS classification is required.
e.rationale: Both frozen dataset inputs are missing, and public data do not distinguish commodity beet growers from firms providing a recurring outsourced operating service. Neither the LMM denominator nor an eligible numerator can be bounded without replacing the declared dataset gap.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate separates generative AI from mechanization and autonomous equipment.
The accountable outsourced-operator population itself remains unverified.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on autonomous beet operations, landowner or processor insourcing, full-farm outsourcing, custom-operator substitution, processor integration, and changes in responsibility for storage and delivery would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Beet production requires an accountable operator for field access, agronomy, specialized equipment, timing, processor coordination, storage quality, safety, and delivery. AI and autonomous equipment can reduce labor, while landowners or processors may self-operate or substitute task-specific contractors for a full-service operator.

caveats: Crop purchase contracts are not equivalent to outsourced-service billing.
Policy-supported sugar pricing and cooperative patronage can obscure sharing of operating efficiencies.
q.change_evidence: Verified full-farm operating agreements showing fixed fees, crop shares, cost reimbursement, input pass-through, processor bonuses, renewal cadence, and customer concentration are required.
q.rationale: No verified outsourced operating-service population or representative operating agreement was found. Common sugar-beet contracts coordinate crop production and sale to processors, so beet prices and grower margins do not reveal contractual retention of a labor benefit under the fixed lens.

caveats: No five-year AI implementation study specific to sugar-beet farming was found.
Adoption and implementation vary sharply by farm scale and processor technology requirements.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-season beet-farm pilots reporting adoption, labor hours, input use, yield, sucrose recovery, storage shrink, forecast error, integration cost, and operator override rates would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Planning, agronomic decision support, processor coordination, recordkeeping, procurement, and maintenance knowledge can be implemented within five years, but rural connectivity, seasonal peaks, crop and storage quality risk, equipment integration, farm scale, and operator trust constrain adoption.

caveats: Farm succession does not necessarily transfer a recurring customer-service business.
Processor delivery rights and cooperative interests may not transfer with operating control.
s5.change_evidence: After establishing eligible firms, principal-operator ages, land and operating-company ownership, cooperative and processor rights, succession plans, lease terms, broker mandates, and five-year control transactions would be required.
s5.rationale: No defensible eligible LMM outsourced-operator population was identified, so a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability cannot be separated from land sales, family farm succession, processor cooperative membership, or ordinary farm asset transfers.

principal_driver: Recurring processor demand for domestic sugar beets within a coordinated and policy-supported sugar supply chain.
principal_weakness: Processor crop contracts do not establish an outsourced operating-service model, and no defensible LMM firm population is available.

sources.supports: The industry boundary as establishments primarily engaged in growing sugar beets.

sources.supports: Evidence that production and marketing contracts are a primary coordination method for sugar beets and that contract use rises with farm size.

sources.supports: Field-crop adoption of guidance, mapping, and variable-rate technologies and evidence on labor-saving and farm-size effects.
sources.title: Precision Agriculture in the Digital Era: Recent Adoption on U.S. Farms

sources.supports: USDA occupation shares and wages for equipment operators, crop workers, supervisors, managers, and other agricultural labor.
sources.title: Farm Labor

sources.supports: Broad occupation-level GenAI exposure and the distinction between technical exposure, augmentation, and realized automation.
sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A global analysis of potential effects on job quantity and quality

title: Sugar Beet Farming
